content_html:
In the early 1980s, the personal computer market was a raucous bazaar, teeming with variety. Each distinct type of personal computer—from Atari, Radio Shack, Apple, Commodore, Texas Instruments, and many more—had its own sub-culture of clubs and magazines, full of partisans ready to tout the superiority of their chosen machines.[1]

Though a substantial bloc of smaller manufactures such as North Star and Vector Graphic all supported an Altair-compatible hardware standard (8080-compatible processor and S-100 bus) and Gary Kildall’s CP/M operating system, they were far from a monopoly share. Standards in graphics, sound, and peripheral connections varied even more widely. Most microcomputers used processors compatible with Intel’s 8080 or MOS Technology’s 6502, but the BASIC programming language was the only near-universal lingua franca, often pre-installed in read-only-memory (ROM) chips on the computer and usually supplied by Microsoft.

Over the course of the 1980s, the IBM PC and its successors paved over all of that, leaving behind a near-uniform cookie-cutter subdivision of compatible computers. By the end of the decade only fragmentary remains attested to the lost world that preceded it: the antiquated Apple IIs still populating school computer labs; the strange boxes at the electronics store labeled “Amiga” and “Atari ST” (the last gasps of dying giants); the Macintoshes found in the offices of graphic designers, writers, publishers, and other members of the cultural avant garde.

The full story of how that unfolded will have to wait for another volume. For now, we will see how it began, with the launch of the IBM Personal Computer in 1981. Ironically, the ecosystem was closed by openness.

Project Chess

Ever since Thomas Waston, Junior launched International Business Machines (IBM) into the electronic computing market in the 1950s, the company had become nearly synonymous with the idea of the computer. Though many of their offerings were technically impressive, especially the breakthrough System/360 line launched in 1965, their success rested mainly on the comprehensiveness of their product line and their unmatched sales and service departments. Though not quite so dominant as it had been in prior decades, the IBM of 1980, with 40% of the global computer market, was still a powerhouse—the popular press routinely called it a “colossus.” To other computer makers, it was simply “the environment”; to be in the business was to swim in IBM’s sea.[2]

IBM’s greatest strength lay in large, mainframe computers. It could not dominate everywhere along the ever-expanding digital frontier, swarming with upstarts staking out new territories for minicomputers, software, dedicated word processing systems, and more. Each market segment had its own leading companies—smaller than IBM individually, but collectively a grave threat to its position as the center of gravity of the computer business. When it came to the very small computers that were selling in the hundreds of thousands by 1980 and projected to continue growing at an astonishing 40% per year, IBM had nothing at all to show for itself.[3]

IBM leadership had known since the early 1970s that the plummeting cost of electronics would make it practical to dedicate an entire computer to a single user. The problem was translating that knowledge into a product that would reach a mass audience. In 1973, IBM engineer Paul Friedel launched a single-user computer project that became the IBM 5100, a fifty-pound, all-in-one computer and terminal for scientists and engineers. In 1983, its creator tried to claim the 5100 as the “missing link” in personal computer history, but with a starting price of $9,000, it hardly belongs in the same forest as the Altair, let alone in its family tree.[4]

The 5100 did not have a microprocessor (which most computer engineers in 1973 still considered far too feeble an instrument to power a real computer). It ran on a multi-chip processor developed out of the IBM office in Boca Raton, Florida, about an hour north of Miami, which housed IBM’s Entry Level Systems Division. As the personal computer rose and rose in the second half of the 1970s, it became obvious in Boca Raton (and even in the airy heights of IBM headquarters in Armonk, New York) that to maintain its reputation as a leader in the world of computing, IBM had to respond. In 1978, the Florida office started work on the System 23/Datamaster, a true microcomputer based on the 8-bit Intel 8085 (a variant of the popular 8080). But two years into that project, it remained mired in development, weighed down by a succession of changes mandated by leadership to align the product with other parts of the company. It was also obvious based on its cost structure that the Datamaster would cost far more than leading personal computers like the Apple II and TRS-80.[5]

Forty-year-old Boca Raton executive Bill Lowe was so disgusted with this state of affairs that in July 1980 he went before the corporate management committee in Armonk and declared that IBM was culturally incapable of producing a personal computer; they would have to slap the IBM logo on a white-label device from the likes of Apple or Atari to bring anything to market. This may have been a gambit intended to force corporate to give him a free hand, but it was not all bluster; there had certainly been talks about selling an Atari computer under IBM livery.[6]

Whether in accordance with Lowe’s plan or not, IBM CEO Frank Cary refused to accept that there was any kind of computer that IBMers couldn’t build. He “decided that the assumptions behind these machines [the 5100 and Datamaster] were the problem. His executives were all assuming that the systems would use only IBM technology, that they’d be sold only by IBM salesmen, that they’d carry all the standard IBM service policies.” An internal study had found that procuring the physical parts alone for a personal computer through the normal IBM channels would cost more than the computer’s target retail price. Cary’s solution was to give Lowe carte blanche to develop a personal computer within a newly invented corporate structure, the Independent Business Unit. Lowe would report directly to the CEO and be shielded from all corporate politics. He had one month to come back with a proposal that would deliver a saleable computer in one year. Internally, the initiative was called Project Chess and the device the Acorn; externally IBM would call it simply the IBM Personal Computer (PC).[7]

Lowe, a corporate climber who had been moving up the IBM ladder since 1962, soon saw the chance to grab another rung: the sudden heart attack of the head of the Rochester, Minnesota office put him in line for a Vice Presidency. He quickly dropped Project Chess, which had already served his purposes by raising his profile with corporate leadership, to pursue this new opportunity. Responsibility for the development of the IBM personal computer thus fell into the lap of Don Estridge.[8]

Slightly older than Lowe, Estridge appeared to have a much less bright future at the company. After stints on the SAGE air defense project and the Apollo project, he eagerly put down roots at the newly opened Boca Raton office in 1969, back in his native state of Florida. Rebelling against the “I’ve Been Moved” company culture, he had no interest in leaving home again to chase promotions. He was also coming off of a rocky “Series 1” minicomputer project that had nearly sunk his career. It was salvaged from financial ruin only by a lucky order from State Farm Insurance.[9]

Nonetheless, Estridge had two qualities that made him perfectly suited to bring Project Chess to fruition. First, the fact that he held the norms of IBM management culture in low regard. He wielded his direct line to the CEO as a talisman to ward off vice-presidential demands and bureaucratic process, and in so doing prevented the lightning sprint to a 1981 launch from bogging down into a multi-year slog. Second, he had insight into the target market because he belonged to it: he had bought an Apple II in 1977 and, like thousands of his fellow hobbyists, tinkered on it in the evenings after work.[10]

Estridge protected his people from pressure to use only the IBM dealer network and IBM suppliers. They would sell the PC through third-party retailers—most importantly, ComputerLand. And, though IBM factories were allowed to put in their bids like everyone else, the Acorn would be built mostly from third-party parts: disk drives from Tandon, power supplies from Zenith, printers from Epson, and a processor from Intel. Even after the release of the PC, internal IBM suppliers would be forced to compete with outside vendors for business: to bootstrap an ecosystem as quickly as possible, Project Chess would follow in the footsteps of Apple and publish all the technical specs needed to allow and encourage third parties to write software and create hardware accessories for their new computer.[11]

Intel was a natural choice for the processor, because many of the engineers in Boca Raton had already become familiar with Intel hardware from the Datamaster project’s 8085. But Estridge’s hardware team wanted to move up to a sixteen-bit processor from the eight-bit processor of the Datamaster (and the vast majority of microcomputers than in use). Doing calculations in sixteen-bit chunks would make the Acorn 1 more efficient at data processing and (most importantly) allow it to support larger amounts of memory. Intel offered the fully-sixteen-bit 8086, but the Chess team chose the hybrid 8088, which processed sixteen-bit chunks internally but communicated with the external word (over its “data bus”) eight bits at a time. This would substantially reduce the overall cost of the machine, since eight-bit support chips were much more common and less expensive to produce.[12]

From CP/M to MS-DOS

Even as they were settling these major hardware problems, the Chess team began its search for software. Jack Sams, tasked with heading up this effort, wanted the Acorn to support two modes of operation, both very popular in the personal computing world: running BASIC from read-only memory (ROM) for simple programming tasks and games, and running a disk-based operating system like CP/M from Digital Research for word-processing, accounting, and heavy data-processing tasks.[13]

Sams was tasked with finding suppliers for this software, and he went first to a name he already knew and trusted: Microsoft, which provided the BASICs installed on most of the major brand names in the personal computer market. In August 1980, he met with Microsoft’s principals, Bill Gates and Paul Allen, in Seattle. Aware that aligning themselves with IBM could prove very lucrative, they eagerly agreed to supply their BASIC and any other software IBM might require (they ultimately provided several other programming language packages as well as Microsoft’s version of the increasingly musty Colossal Cave Adventure). When asked about licensing CP/M, however, Gates had to regretfully decline. Microsoft had no rights to CP/M, but he was happy to phone up Gary Kildall at Digital Research in Pacific Grove, California, and arrange a meeting.[14]

Much has been made of this fateful meeting, on August 22, 1980, to which Kildall, unlike the solicitous Gates and Allen, arrived hours late. The negotiations about licensing the yet-to-be-released sixteen-bit CP/M-86 thus fell to his business manager (and wife) Dorothy Kildall. Though the events of that day are much disputed, it is certain that everyone walked away unhappy, and no deal was reached. But IBM had no one else to turn to, and so negotiations continued. The ultimate outcome (a triumph for Microsoft and the eclipse of Digital Research) was determined not by the events of that day, but by the predispositions of the three main parties.[15]

Sams disliked the standoffish attitude of Digital Research and had wanted, in the first place, to deal only with the supplier he already trusted: Microsoft. As for Gates and Kildall, though they both loved computers, and had once briefly considered a merger of their companies, they had very different personalities. Gates had been angling to build a business since he was in high school; he and Allen created Microsoft to meet a market need: a BASIC interpreter for the Altair, which they believe would sell like hotcakes. Kildall had created CP/M as the incidental byproduct of a hobby interest: his desired to write PL/M code on a microcomputer. For better or worse, he lacked Gates’ relentlessness and drive. He certainly wanted to do well for himself, but he cared more about building interesting software than winning in the market, and he disliked IBM’s attitude more than he liked their money. The Digital Research of the 1980s would never have become the Microsoft of the 1990s, no matter what happened on August 22nd.

The facts of how these dynamics played out were as follows: a frustrated Sams went back to Gates to ask again about a disk operating system. Through Allen, Gates knew of one other possibility: a 16-bit CP/M clone created by a local company, Seattle Computer Products (SCP). In 1979, SCP had started selling an S-100 compatible motherboard for the Intel 8086 processor, designed by employee Tim Paterson. But unlike in the ancient of the Altair, by 1979 most hobbyists expected to have a disk operating system available for their computer. So, Paterson wrote one, which he called Quick and Dirty Operating System (QDOS), and which SCP sold externally as 86-DOS. It was a near-perfect clone of CP/M for the 8086. Because the 8086 and 8088 used identical machine code, this meant it would also work on the IBM personal computer.[16]

Gates offered to put Sams in touch with SCP, but the dealings with Digital Research had already exhausted his patience with microcomputer software makers. He asked Gates to do the deal himself and license 86-DOS to IBM. Gates duly did so (all the while concealing the existence of his deep-pocketed customer from SCP) and then worked diligently to make sure Microsoft could deliver exactly what IBM wanted, in plenty of time for the 1981 PC launch—he even hired Paterson away from SCP to polish 86-DOS into what IBM would call PC-DOS. But the crucial moment for Microsoft came in July 1981, when Gates convinced SCP’s owner to sell him all the rights to 86-DOS, in exchange for $50,000 and a perpetual license back to SCP. Because IBM had not secured an exclusive license to PC-DOS, Microsoft could now sell it to others, giving this same piece of software its fourth name, MS-DOS. This deal was the basis for Microsoft’s future multi-billion-dollar empire.[17]

With Digital Research threatening to sue over the similarities between PC-DOS and CP/M-86, IBM agreed to offer the latter as an additional operating system for the IBM PC. But this alternative did not actually become available until April 1982, months after IBM launched its computer, and because Digital Research demanded per-copy royalties (while Microsoft licensed DOS for a flat fee), it sold for $240, six times as much as PC-DOS. This was not the end of Digital Research, which found a niche in the operating system market and survived until 1991, when another software maker, Novell offered $80 million to buy the company. The previous quarter, Microsoft had made $140 million in profit.[18]

The Arrival

The IBM PC debuted on August 12, 1981, in a press conference at New York’s Waldorf Astoria hotel, almost exactly a year after Lowe had committed to delivering it. The Chess team had estimated one million units in sales in the first three years, but corporate planning, unable to credit such a figure, cut the target down to 200,000, which restricted supply in the back half of 1981. Nonetheless, after three years, the PC was right on target for one million sold. Estridge rose from obscurity to become an overnight star: having “delivered nearly $1 billion of revenue, that, not being in anyone’s business plan, appeared to come out of thin air… Estridge’s bosses looked at him as something of a magician.”[19]

An ungenerous historian might credit the PC’s success solely to the IBM name: businesspeople skeptical of the microcomputer fad (or uncertain whether existing brands could be trusted to last) felt comfortable betting on a machine to which this venerable, trusted brand had lent its credit. And there is certainly truth to that. As soon as he heard the IBM PC was coming, Martin Alpert, president of a small technology company in Cleveland, shifted his whole business on making add-ons for the computer. He spoke for third-party vendors as a whole when he said that “people wanted a standard. They didn’t know what to buy until they heard about the IBM PC and they figured they couldn’t go wrong because it was IBM. As soon as we saw the machine, we believed in it and we bet the future of our company on it.”[20]

Yet it is also true that the PC would never have succeeded so well had it not also been an excellent, forward-looking design, which combined many of the best features of its competitors. As BYTE’s senior editor wrote, it had “color graphics like the Apple II, an 80-column display like the TRS-80 Model II, a redefinable character set like the Atari 800, a 16-bit microprocessor like the Texas Instruments TI 99/4, an expanded memory space like the Apple III, a full-function uppercase and lowercase keyboard like the TRS-80 Model III, and BASIC color graphics like the TRS-80 Color Computer…”[21]

The most important of these features were the sixteen-bit microprocessor and the expanded memory access, which went hand in hand. Most 8-bit computers could read from or write to a maximum of sixty-four kilobytes of memory (by combining two eight-bit addresses). The Apple III had quadrupled this with a technique called bank-switching, but the IBM, with sixteen-bit addresses plus a four-bit segment identifier, could access an entire megabyte (though applications could access only 640 kilobytes—later a notorious number—with the rest reserved for system software and input/output devices). One would be hard-pressed to find a microcomputer user in possession of so much memory in 1981, but with prices falling and application software growing ever more sophisticated, business users would soon both want that much memory and be able to afford it, and the PC was well-prepared for that eventuality.[22]

By the spring of 1983, IBM’s personal computer sales already matched those of Apple and Tandy. Andrew Pollack of the New York Times noted that “an entire subindustry has sprung up to supply parts, accessories and advice for the I.B.M. personal computer,” and that “I.B.M.’s role in the personal computer world is beginning to resemble its central role in the mainframe computer business, in which I.B.M. is the sun around which everything else revolves.” A new catalog of IBM-specific literature sprung up to serve this new market: David Bunnell, who had left MITS (creator of the ALtair) to found Personal Computing, now became editor-in-chief for PC Magazine, entirely dedicated to IBM’s new computer. Many brands (from Dumpster to Kleenex) had found their proprietary name transformed into a generic label, but IBM had managed to turn the previously generic term “personal computer” into the proprietary acronym “PC.”[23]

The PC’s open architecture, borrowed from minicomputers via Apple and the Altair-compatibles, attracted software and hardware vendors and kicked off a new flywheel of success even more powerful than the one that propelled the Apple II. As much from necessity as from calculation, Estridge’s team had blended the open traditions of the personal computer and the authority of IBM’s imprimatur into an enticing cocktail. Both conformists and rebels could find something appealing in its flavor. The marketing campaign struck the same chords: “My own IBM computer. Imagine that,” went one tagline. Now you could have complete control over your own computer without giving up the assurance of the IBM brand.[24]

Yet, this concoction that so enchanted its drinkers also proved dangerous to the brewer. An open architecture was more easily copied, and by March 1983, more than 20 “clones” of the IBM PC already existed, “interchangeable or almost interchangeable with the I.B.M. computer.” Internally, IBM executives seethed at the press focus on Don Estridge, who generate only a small fraction of IBM’s revenue while hurting the rest of the company “by not trying to tie his products into the rest of the company’s, by not working with the IBM sales force, or by not being willing to buy his parts from IBM.” In short order, the full force of internal politics was unleashed on the formerly sheltered PC group. The immediate successor models to the PC, 1983’s XT and 1984’s AT, both sold terrifically, but darker times loomed ahead. After its magician died in a horrific plane crash in Dallas in 1985, IBM would dig deep into its hat but never produce another rabbit.[25]

The full story of IBM’s downfall will have to wait for another series. But in the next post we will look at the consequences of the IBM PC’s arrival for the rest of the industry.

First, the fact that he held the norms of IBM management culture in low regard. He wielded his direct line to the CEO as a talisman to ward off vice-presidential demands and bureaucratic process, and in so doing prevented the lightning sprint to a 1981 launch from bogging down into a multi-year slog. Second, he had insight into the target market because he belonged to it: he had bought an Apple II in 1977 and, like thousands of his fellow hobbyists, tinkered on it in the evenings after work.[10] Estridge protected his people from pressure to use only the IBM dealer network and IBM suppliers. They would sell the PC through third-party retailers—most importantly, ComputerLand. And, though IBM factories were allowed to put in their bids like everyone else, the Acorn would be built mostly from third-party parts: disk drives from Tandon, power supplies from Zenith, printers from Epson, and a processor from Intel. Even after the release of the PC, internal IBM suppliers would be forced to compete with outside vendors for business: to bootstrap an ecosystem as quickly as possible, Project Chess would follow in the footsteps of Apple and publish all the technical specs needed to allow and encourage third parties to write software and create hardware accessories for their new computer.[11] Intel was a natural choice for the processor, because many of the engineers in Boca Raton had already become familiar with Intel hardware from the Datamaster project’s 8085. But Estridge’s hardware team wanted to move up to a sixteen-bit processor from the eight-bit processor of the Datamaster (and the vast majority of microcomputers than in use). Doing calculations in sixteen-bit chunks would make the Acorn 1 more efficient at data processing and (most importantly) allow it to support larger amounts of memory. Intel offered the fully-sixteen-bit 8086, but the Chess team chose the hybrid 8088, which processed sixteen-bit chunks internally but communicated with the external word (over its “data bus”) eight bits at a time. This would substantially reduce the overall cost of the machine, since eight-bit support chips were much more common and less expensive to produce.[12] From CP/M to MS-DOS Even as they were settling these major hardware problems, the Chess team began its search for software. Jack Sams, tasked with heading up this effort, wanted the Acorn to support two modes of operation, both very popular in the personal computing world: running BASIC from read-only memory (ROM) for simple programming tasks and games, and running a disk-based operating system like CP/M from Digital Research for word-processing, accounting, and heavy data-processing tasks.[13] Sams was tasked with finding suppliers for this software, and he went first to a name he already knew and trusted: Microsoft, which provided the BASICs installed on most of the major brand names in the personal computer market. In August 1980, he met with Microsoft’s principals, Bill Gates and Paul Allen, in Seattle. Aware that aligning themselves with IBM could prove very lucrative, they eagerly agreed to supply their BASIC and any other software IBM might require (they ultimately provided several other programming language packages as well as Microsoft’s version of the increasingly musty Colossal Cave Adventure). When asked about licensing CP/M, however, Gates had to regretfully decline. Microsoft had no rights to CP/M, but he was happy to phone up Gary Kildall at Digital Research in Pacific Grove, California, and arrange a meeting.[14] The house—at the time, Digital Research headquarters—where the meeting on August 22, 1980 took place. [Piotr J. Flatau / CC BY-SA 4.0] Much has been made of this fateful meeting, on August 22, 1980, to which Kildall, unlike the solicitous Gates and Allen, arrived hours late. The negotiations about licensing the yet-to-be-released sixteen-bit CP/M-86 thus fell to his business manager (and wife) Dorothy Kildall. Though the events of that day are much disputed, it is certain that everyone walked away unhappy, and no deal was reached. But IBM had no one else to turn to, and so negotiations continued. The ultimate outcome (a triumph for Microsoft and the eclipse of Digital Research) was determined not by the events of that day, but by the predispositions of the three main parties.[15] Sams disliked the standoffish attitude of Digital Research and had wanted, in the first place, to deal only with the supplier he already trusted: Microsoft. As for Gates and Kildall, though they both loved computers, and had once briefly considered a merger of their companies, they had very different personalities. Gates had been angling to build a business since he was in high school; he and Allen created Microsoft to meet a market need: a BASIC interpreter for the Altair, which they believe would sell like hotcakes. Kildall had created CP/M as the incidental byproduct of a hobby interest: his desired to write PL/M code on a microcomputer. For better or worse, he lacked Gates’ relentlessness and drive. He certainly wanted to do well for himself, but he cared more about building interesting software than winning in the market, and he disliked IBM’s attitude more than he liked their money. The Digital Research of the 1980s would never have become the Microsoft of the 1990s, no matter what happened on August 22nd. The facts of how these dynamics played out were as follows: a frustrated Sams went back to Gates to ask again about a disk operating system. Through Allen, Gates knew of one other possibility: a 16-bit CP/M clone created by a local company, Seattle Computer Products (SCP). In 1979, SCP had started selling an S-100 compatible motherboard for the Intel 8086 processor, designed by employee Tim Paterson. But unlike in the ancient of the Altair, by 1979 most hobbyists expected to have a disk operating system available for their computer. So, Paterson wrote one, which he called Quick and Dirty Operating System (QDOS), and which SCP sold externally as 86-DOS. It was a near-perfect clone of CP/M for the 8086. Because the 8086 and 8088 used identical machine code, this meant it would also work on the IBM personal computer.[16] Gates offered to put Sams in touch with SCP, but the dealings with Digital Research had already exhausted his patience with microcomputer software makers. He asked Gates to do the deal himself and license 86-DOS to IBM. Gates duly did so (all the while concealing the existence of his deep-pocketed customer from SCP) and then worked diligently to make sure Microsoft could deliver exactly what IBM wanted, in plenty of time for the 1981 PC launch—he even hired Paterson away from SCP to polish 86-DOS into what IBM would call PC-DOS. But the crucial moment for Microsoft came in July 1981, when Gates convinced SCP’s owner to sell him all the rights to 86-DOS, in exchange for $50,000 and a perpetual license back to SCP. Because IBM had not secured an exclusive license to PC-DOS, Microsoft could now sell it to others, giving this same piece of software its fourth name, MS-DOS. This deal was the basis for Microsoft’s future multi-billion-dollar empire.[17] Listing files on PC-DOS With Digital Research threatening to sue over the similarities between PC-DOS and CP/M-86, IBM agreed to offer the latter as an additional operating system for the IBM PC. But this alternative did not actually become available until April 1982, months after IBM launched its computer, and because Digital Research demanded per-copy royalties (while Microsoft licensed DOS for a flat fee), it sold for $240, six times as much as PC-DOS. This was not the end of Digital Research, which found a niche in the operating system market and survived until 1991, when another software maker, Novell offered $80 million to buy the company. The previous quarter, Microsoft had made $140 million in profit.[18] The Arrival The IBM PC debuted on August 12, 1981, in a press conference at New York’s Waldorf Astoria hotel, almost exactly a year after Lowe had committed to delivering it. The Chess team had estimated one million units in sales in the first three years, but corporate planning, unable to credit such a figure, cut the target down to 200,000, which restricted supply in the back half of 1981. Nonetheless, after three years, the PC was right on target for one million sold. Estridge rose from obscurity to become an overnight star: having “delivered nearly $1 billion of revenue, that, not being in anyone’s business plan, appeared to come out of thin air… Estridge’s bosses looked at him as something of a magician.”[19] AN IBM PC configured with two disk drives. [Dpbsmith / CC BY-SA 3.0] An ungenerous historian might credit the PC’s success solely to the IBM name: businesspeople skeptical of the microcomputer fad (or uncertain whether existing brands could be trusted to last) felt comfortable betting on a machine to which this venerable, trusted brand had lent its credit. And there is certainly truth to that. As soon as he heard the IBM PC was coming, Martin Alpert, president of a small technology company in Cleveland, shifted his whole business on making add-ons for the computer. He spoke for third-party vendors as a whole when he said that “people wanted a standard. They didn’t know what to buy until they heard about the IBM PC and they figured they couldn’t go wrong because it was IBM. As soon as we saw the machine, we believed in it and we bet the future of our company on it.”[20] Yet it is also true that the PC would never have succeeded so well had it not also been an excellent, forward-looking design, which combined many of the best features of its competitors. As BYTE’s senior editor wrote, it had “color graphics like the Apple II, an 80-column display like the TRS-80 Model II, a redefinable character set like the Atari 800, a 16-bit microprocessor like the Texas Instruments TI 99/4, an expanded memory space like the Apple III, a full-function uppercase and lowercase keyboard like the TRS-80 Model III, and BASIC color graphics like the TRS-80 Color Computer…”[21] The most important of these features were the sixteen-bit microprocessor and the expanded memory access, which went hand in hand. Most 8-bit computers could read from or write to a maximum of sixty-four kilobytes of memory (by combining two eight-bit addresses). The Apple III had quadrupled this with a technique called bank-switching, but the IBM, with sixteen-bit addresses plus a four-bit segment identifier, could access an entire megabyte (though applications could access only 640 kilobytes—later a notorious number—with the rest reserved for system software and input/output devices). One would be hard-pressed to find a microcomputer user in possession of so much memory in 1981, but with prices falling and application software growing ever more sophisticated, business users would soon both want that much memory and be able to afford it, and the PC was well-prepared for that eventuality.[22] By the spring of 1983, IBM’s personal computer sales already matched those of Apple and Tandy. Andrew Pollack of the New York Times noted that “an entire subindustry has sprung up to supply parts, accessories and advice for the I.B.M. personal computer,” and that “I.B.M.’s role in the personal computer world is beginning to resemble its central role in the mainframe computer business, in which I.B.M. is the sun around which everything else revolves.” A new catalog of IBM-specific literature sprung up to serve this new market: David Bunnell, who had left MITS (creator of the ALtair) to found Personal Computing, now became editor-in-chief for PC Magazine, entirely dedicated to IBM’s new computer. Many brands (from Dumpster to Kleenex) had found their proprietary name transformed into a generic label, but IBM had managed to turn the previously generic term “personal computer” into the proprietary acronym “PC.”[23] The cover of the inaugural issue of PC Magazine, which reached shelves in January 1982. The PC’s open architecture, borrowed from minicomputers via Apple and the Altair-compatibles, attracted software and hardware vendors and kicked off a new flywheel of success even more powerful than the one that propelled the Apple II. As much from necessity as from calculation, Estridge’s team had blended the open traditions of the personal computer and the authority of IBM’s imprimatur into an enticing cocktail. Both conformists and rebels could find something appealing in its flavor. The marketing campaign struck the same chords: “My own IBM computer. Imagine that,” went one tagline. Now you could have complete control over your own computer without giving up the assurance of the IBM brand.[24] Yet, this concoction that so enchanted its drinkers also proved dangerous to the brewer. An open architecture was more easily copied, and by March 1983, more than 20 “clones” of the IBM PC already existed, “interchangeable or almost interchangeable with the I.B.M. computer.” Internally, IBM executives seethed at the press focus on Don Estridge, who generate only a small fraction of IBM’s revenue while hurting the rest of the company “by not trying to tie his products into the rest of the company’s, by not working with the IBM sales force, or by not being willing to buy his parts from IBM.” In short order, the full force of internal politics was unleashed on the formerly sheltered PC group. The immediate successor models to the PC, 1983’s XT and 1984’s AT, both sold terrifically, but darker times loomed ahead. After its magician died in a horrific plane crash in Dallas in 1985, IBM would dig deep into its hat but never produce another rabbit.[25] The full story of IBM’s downfall will have to wait for another series. But in the next post we will look at the consequences of the IBM PC’s arrival for the rest of the industry. blocks.html_en: In the early 1980s, the personal computer market was a raucous bazaar, teeming with variety. Each distinct type of personal computer—from Atari, Radio Shack, Apple, Commodore, Texas Instruments, and many more—had its own sub-culture of clubs and magazines, full of partisans ready to tout the superiority of their chosen machines.[1]

sentences.en: In the early 1980s, the personal computer market was a raucous bazaar, teeming with variety.
sentences.zh: 20 世纪 80 年代初，个人电脑市场是一个喧闹的集市，品种繁多。

sentences.en: Each distinct type of personal computer—from Atari, Radio Shack, Apple, Commodore, Texas Instruments, and many more—had its own sub-culture of clubs and magazines, full of partisans ready to tout the superiority of their chosen machines.[1]
sentences.zh: 每种不同类型的个人电脑——来自 Atari、Radio Shack、Apple、Commodore、Texas Instruments 等等——都有自己的俱乐部和杂志亚文化，其中充满了随时准备吹捧他们所选择的机器的优越性的党派。 [1]

blocks.html_en: Though a substantial bloc of smaller manufactures such as North Star and Vector Graphic all supported an Altair-compatible hardware standard (8080-compatible processor and S-100 bus) and Gary Kildall’s CP/M operating system, they were far from a monopoly share. Standards in graphics, sound, and peripheral connections varied even more widely. Most microcomputers used processors compatible with Intel’s 8080 or MOS Technology’s 6502, but the BASIC programming language was the only near-universal lingua franca, often pre-installed in read-only-memory (ROM) chips on the computer and usually supplied by Microsoft.

sentences.en: Though a substantial bloc of smaller manufactures such as North Star and Vector Graphic all supported an Altair-compatible hardware standard (8080-compatible processor and S-100 bus) and Gary Kildall’s CP/M operating system, they were far from a monopoly share.
sentences.zh: 尽管 North Star 和 Vector Graphic 等大量小型制造商都支持 Altair 兼容的硬件标准（8080 兼容的处理器和 S-100 总线）和 Gary Kildall 的 CP/M 操作系统，但它们远未形成垄断份额。

sentences.en: Standards in graphics, sound, and peripheral connections varied even more widely.
sentences.zh: 图形、声音和外围连接方面的标准差异更大。

sentences.en: Most microcomputers used processors compatible with Intel’s 8080 or MOS Technology’s 6502, but the BASIC programming language was the only near-universal lingua franca, often pre-installed in read-only-memory (ROM) chips on the computer and usually supplied by Microsoft.
sentences.zh: 大多数微型计算机使用与英特尔 8080 或 MOS Technology 6502 兼容的处理器，但 BASIC 编程语言是唯一近乎通用的通用语言，通常预装在计算机的只读存储器 (ROM) 芯片中，通常由微软提供。

sentences.en: Table of Contents from The Microcomputer Handbook: A Buyer’s Guide (1982, edited by Dick Olney).
sentences.zh: 《微型计算机手册：购买者指南》（1982 年，迪克·奥尔尼编辑）的目录。

sentences.en: Most of the listed computers are mutually incompatible (i.e. software written for one could not run on another).
sentences.zh: 大多数列出的计算机相互不兼容（即为一台计算机编写的软件无法在另一台计算机上运行）。

blocks.html_en: Over the course of the 1980s, the IBM PC and its successors paved over all of that, leaving behind a near-uniform cookie-cutter subdivision of compatible computers. By the end of the decade only fragmentary remains attested to the lost world that preceded it: the antiquated Apple IIs still populating school computer labs; the strange boxes at the electronics store labeled “Amiga” and “Atari ST” (the last gasps of dying giants); the Macintoshes found in the offices of graphic designers, writers, publishers, and other members of the cultural avant garde.

sentences.en: Over the course of the 1980s, the IBM PC and its successors paved over all of that, leaving behind a near-uniform cookie-cutter subdivision of compatible computers.
sentences.zh: 在 20 世纪 80 年代，IBM PC 及其后继者奠定了这一切的基础，留下了几乎统一的兼容计算机的千篇一律的细分市场。

sentences.en: By the end of the decade only fragmentary remains attested to the lost world that preceded it: the antiquated Apple IIs still populating school computer labs; the strange boxes at the electronics store labeled “Amiga” and “Atari ST” (the last gasps of dying giants); the Macintoshes found in the offices of graphic designers, writers, publishers, and other members of the cultural avant garde.
sentences.zh: 到本世纪末，只有零碎的证据仍然证明了之前的失落世界：过时的 Apple II 仍然存在于学校计算机实验室中；电子商店里标有“Amiga”和“Atari ST”（垂死巨人的最后一口气）的奇怪盒子；图形设计师、作家、出版商和其他文化先锋派成员的办公室里都有麦金塔电脑。

blocks.html_en: The full story of how that unfolded will have to wait for another volume. For now, we will see how it began, with the launch of the IBM Personal Computer in 1981. Ironically, the ecosystem was closed by openness.

sentences.en: The full story of how that unfolded will have to wait for another volume.
sentences.zh: 整个故事如何展开将不得不等待另一卷。

sentences.en: For now, we will see how it began, with the launch of the IBM Personal Computer in 1981.
sentences.zh: 现在，我们将了解它是如何开始的，即 1981 年 IBM 个人计算机的推出。

sentences.en: Ironically, the ecosystem was closed by openness.
sentences.zh: 具有讽刺意味的是，生态系统因开放而封闭。

sentences.en: Project Chess
sentences.zh: 国际象棋项目

blocks.html_en: Ever since Thomas Waston, Junior launched International Business Machines (IBM) into the electronic computing market in the 1950s, the company had become nearly synonymous with the idea of the computer. Though many of their offerings were technically impressive, especially the breakthrough System/360 line launched in 1965, their success rested mainly on the comprehensiveness of their product line and their unmatched sales and service departments. Though not quite so dominant as it had been in prior decades, the IBM of 1980, with 40% of the global computer market, was still a powerhouse—the popular press routinely called it a “colossus.” To other computer makers, it was simply “the environment”; to be in the business was to swim in IBM’s sea.[2]

sentences.en: Ever since Thomas Waston, Junior launched International Business Machines (IBM) into the electronic computing market in the 1950s, the company had become nearly synonymous with the idea of the computer.
sentences.zh: 自从 Thomas Waston, Junior 在 20 世纪 50 年代将国际商业机器公司 (IBM) 引入电子计算市场以来，该公司几乎成为计算机理念的代名词。

sentences.en: Though many of their offerings were technically impressive, especially the breakthrough System/360 line launched in 1965, their success rested mainly on the comprehensiveness of their product line and their unmatched sales and service departments.
sentences.zh: 尽管他们的许多产品在技术上令人印象深刻，尤其是 1965 年推出的突破性 System/360 系列，但他们的成功主要取决于产品线的全面性以及无与伦比的销售和服务部门。

sentences.en: Though not quite so dominant as it had been in prior decades, the IBM of 1980, with 40% of the global computer market, was still a powerhouse—the popular press routinely called it a “colossus.”
sentences.zh: 尽管不像前几十年那样占据主导地位，1980 年的 IBM 占据了全球计算机市场 40% 的份额，但仍然是一家强大的公司——大众媒体通常称其为“巨人”。

sentences.en: To other computer makers, it was simply “the environment”; to be in the business was to swim in IBM’s sea.[2]
sentences.zh: 对于其他计算机制造商来说，这只是“环境”；进入这个行业就等于在 IBM 的大海里游泳。[2]

blocks.html_en: IBM’s greatest strength lay in large, mainframe computers. It could not dominate everywhere along the ever-expanding digital frontier, swarming with upstarts staking out new territories for minicomputers, software, dedicated word processing systems, and more. Each market segment had its own leading companies—smaller than IBM individually, but collectively a grave threat to its position as the center of gravity of the computer business. When it came to the very small computers that were selling in the hundreds of thousands by 1980 and projected to continue growing at an astonishing 40% per year, IBM had nothing at all to show for itself.[3]

sentences.en: IBM’s greatest strength lay in large, mainframe computers.
sentences.zh: IBM 的最大优势在于大型主机。

sentences.en: It could not dominate everywhere along the ever-expanding digital frontier, swarming with upstarts staking out new territories for minicomputers, software, dedicated word processing systems, and more.
sentences.zh: 它不可能在不断扩大的数字前沿领域占据主导地位，充斥着新贵，为小型计算机、软件、专用文字处理系统等开辟新领域。

sentences.en: Each market segment had its own leading companies—smaller than IBM individually, but collectively a grave threat to its position as the center of gravity of the computer business.
sentences.zh: 每个细分市场都有自己的领先公司——规模比 IBM 单独小，但总体上对其作为计算机业务重心的地位构成了严重威胁。

sentences.en: When it came to the very small computers that were selling in the hundreds of thousands by 1980 and projected to continue growing at an astonishing 40% per year, IBM had nothing at all to show for itself.[3]
sentences.zh: 当谈到到 1980 年已售出数十万台且预计将以惊人的每年 40% 的速度继续增长的超小型计算机时，IBM 根本没有什么可以展示的。 [3]

blocks.html_en: IBM leadership had known since the early 1970s that the plummeting cost of electronics would make it practical to dedicate an entire computer to a single user. The problem was translating that knowledge into a product that would reach a mass audience. In 1973, IBM engineer Paul Friedel launched a single-user computer project that became the IBM 5100, a fifty-pound, all-in-one computer and terminal for scientists and engineers. In 1983, its creator tried to claim the 5100 as the “missing link” in personal computer history, but with a starting price of $9,000, it hardly belongs in the same forest as the Altair, let alone in its family tree.[4]

sentences.en: IBM leadership had known since the early 1970s that the plummeting cost of electronics would make it practical to dedicate an entire computer to a single user.
sentences.zh: IBM 领导层自 20 世纪 70 年代初就知道，电子产品成本的直线下降将使将整台计算机专用于单个用户变得可行。

sentences.en: The problem was translating that knowledge into a product that would reach a mass audience.
sentences.zh: 问题是将这些知识转化为能够吸引大量受众的产品。

sentences.en: In 1973, IBM engineer Paul Friedel launched a single-user computer project that became the IBM 5100, a fifty-pound, all-in-one computer and terminal for scientists and engineers.
sentences.zh: 1973 年，IBM 工程师 Paul Friedel 启动了一个单用户计算机项目，即 IBM 5100，这是一款为科学家和工程师设计的 50 磅重的一体化计算机和终端。

sentences.en: In 1983, its creator tried to claim the 5100 as the “missing link” in personal computer history, but with a starting price of $9,000, it hardly belongs in the same forest as the Altair, let alone in its family tree.[4]
sentences.zh: 1983 年，它的创建者试图将 5100 称为个人计算机历史上的“缺失的一环”，但它的起价为 9,000 美元，很难与 Altair 属于同一个森林，更不用说在它的家谱中了。 [4]

blocks.html_en: The 5100 did not have a microprocessor (which most computer engineers in 1973 still considered far too feeble an instrument to power a real computer). It ran on a multi-chip processor developed out of the IBM office in Boca Raton, Florida, about an hour north of Miami, which housed IBM’s Entry Level Systems Division. As the personal computer rose and rose in the second half of the 1970s, it became obvious in Boca Raton (and even in the airy heights of IBM headquarters in Armonk, New York) that to maintain its reputation as a leader in the world of computing, IBM had to respond. In 1978, the Florida office started work on the System 23/Datamaster, a true microcomputer based on the 8-bit Intel 8085 (a variant of the popular 8080). But two years into that project, it remained mired in development, weighed down by a succession of changes mandated by leadership to align the product with other parts of the company. It was also obvious based on its cost structure that the Datamaster would cost far more than leading personal computers like the Apple II and TRS-80.[5]

sentences.en: The 5100 did not have a microprocessor (which most computer engineers in 1973 still considered far too feeble an instrument to power a real computer).
sentences.zh: 5100 没有微处理器（1973 年大多数计算机工程师仍然认为微处理器太弱，无法为真正的计算机提供动力）。

sentences.en: It ran on a multi-chip processor developed out of the IBM office in Boca Raton, Florida, about an hour north of Miami, which housed IBM’s Entry Level Systems Division.
sentences.zh: 它运行在由位于佛罗里达州博卡拉顿的 IBM 办公室开发的多芯片处理器上，该办公室距离迈阿密以北约一小时车程，IBM 的入门级系统部门就设在该办公室。

sentences.en: As the personal computer rose and rose in the second half of the 1970s, it became obvious in Boca Raton (and even in the airy heights of IBM headquarters in Armonk, New York) that to maintain its reputation as a leader in the world of computing, IBM had to respond.
sentences.zh: 随着个人计算机在 20 世纪 70 年代后半叶的兴起，在博卡拉顿（甚至在纽约阿蒙克 IBM 总部的空中高处）显而易见的是，为了保持其作为计算领域领导者的声誉，IBM 必须做出回应。

sentences.en: In 1978, the Florida office started work on the System 23/Datamaster, a true microcomputer based on the 8-bit Intel 8085 (a variant of the popular 8080).
sentences.zh: 1978 年，佛罗里达办事处开始开发 System 23/Datamaster，这是一款基于 8 位 Intel 8085（流行的 8080 的变体）的真正微型计算机。

sentences.en: But two years into that project, it remained mired in development, weighed down by a succession of changes mandated by leadership to align the product with other parts of the company.
sentences.zh: 但该项目实施两年后，由于领导层为使产品与公司其他部门保持一致而要求进行的一系列变革，该公司仍深陷开发困境。

sentences.en: It was also obvious based on its cost structure that the Datamaster would cost far more than leading personal computers like the Apple II and TRS-80.[5]
sentences.zh: 从其成本结构来看，很明显 Datamaster 的成本将远远高于 Apple II 和 TRS-80 等领先的个人计算机。 [5]

blocks.html_en: Forty-year-old Boca Raton executive Bill Lowe was so disgusted with this state of affairs that in July 1980 he went before the corporate management committee in Armonk and declared that IBM was culturally incapable of producing a personal computer; they would have to slap the IBM logo on a white-label device from the likes of Apple or Atari to bring anything to market. This may have been a gambit intended to force corporate to give him a free hand, but it was not all bluster; there had certainly been talks about selling an Atari computer under IBM livery.[6]

sentences.en: Forty-year-old Boca Raton executive Bill Lowe was so disgusted with this state of affairs that in July 1980 he went before the corporate management committee in Armonk and declared that IBM was culturally incapable of producing a personal computer; they would have to slap the IBM logo on a white-label device from the likes of Apple or Atari to bring anything to market.
sentences.zh: 40 岁的博卡拉顿高管比尔·洛 (Bill Lowe) 对这种状况非常反感，因此于 1980 年 7 月在阿蒙克 (Armonk) 的公司管理委员会面前宣称 IBM 在文化上没有能力生产个人电脑；他们必须在苹果或雅达利等公司的白标设备上打上 IBM 标志，才能将任何产品推向市场。

sentences.en: This may have been a gambit intended to force corporate to give him a free hand, but it was not all bluster; there had certainly been talks about selling an Atari computer under IBM livery.[6]
sentences.zh: 这可能是一种策略，旨在迫使公司放手让他放手，但这并不全是虚张声势。确实有人讨论过以 IBM 涂装出售 Atari 计算机。 [6]

blocks.html_en: <figcaption>Bill Lowe</figcaption>

sentences.en: Bill Lowe
sentences.zh: 比尔·洛

blocks.html_en: Whether in accordance with Lowe’s plan or not, IBM CEO Frank Cary refused to accept that there was any kind of computer that IBMers couldn’t build. He “decided that the assumptions behind these machines [the 5100 and Datamaster] were the problem. His executives were all assuming that the systems would use only IBM technology, that they’d be sold only by IBM salesmen, that they’d carry all the standard IBM service policies.” An internal study had found that procuring the physical parts alone for a personal computer through the normal IBM channels would cost more than the computer’s target retail price. Cary’s solution was to give Lowe carte blanche to develop a personal computer within a newly invented corporate structure, the Independent Business Unit. Lowe would report directly to the CEO and be shielded from all corporate politics. He had one month to come back with a proposal that would deliver a saleable computer in one year. Internally, the initiative was called Project Chess and the device the Acorn; externally IBM would call it simply the IBM Personal Computer (PC).[7]

sentences.en: Whether in accordance with Lowe’s plan or not, IBM CEO Frank Cary refused to accept that there was any kind of computer that IBMers couldn’t build.
sentences.zh: 无论是否符合 Lowe 的计划，IBM 首席执行官弗兰克·卡里 (Frank Cary) 都拒绝接受 IBM 人无法制造任何类型的计算机的事实。

sentences.en: He “decided that the assumptions behind these machines [the 5100 and Datamaster] were the problem.
sentences.zh: 他“认为这些机器 [5100 和 Datamaster] 背后的假设是问题所在。

sentences.en: His executives were all assuming that the systems would use only IBM technology, that they’d be sold only by IBM salesmen, that they’d carry all the standard IBM service policies.”
sentences.zh: 他的高管们都假设这些系统只会使用 IBM 技术，只会由 IBM 销售人员销售，并且会遵循所有标准的 IBM 服务政策。”

sentences.en: An internal study had found that procuring the physical parts alone for a personal computer through the normal IBM channels would cost more than the computer’s target retail price.
sentences.zh: 一项内部研究发现，通过正常的 IBM 渠道单独采购个人计算机的物理部件的成本将高于计算机的目标零售价。

sentences.en: Cary’s solution was to give Lowe carte blanche to develop a personal computer within a newly invented corporate structure, the Independent Business Unit.
sentences.zh: 卡里的解决方案是全权委托洛在新发明的公司结构“独立业务部门”内开发个人电脑。

sentences.en: Lowe would report directly to the CEO and be shielded from all corporate politics.
sentences.zh: 洛将直接向首席执行官汇报，并免受所有公司政治的影响。

sentences.en: He had one month to come back with a proposal that would deliver a saleable computer in one year.
sentences.zh: 他有一个月的时间提出一项将在一年内交付一台可销售计算机的提案。

sentences.en: Internally, the initiative was called Project Chess and the device the Acorn; externally IBM would call it simply the IBM Personal Computer (PC).[7]
sentences.zh: 在内部，该计划被称为“国际象棋项目”，该设备被称为“橡子”；在外部，IBM 将其简称为 IBM 个人计算机 (PC)。[7]

blocks.html_en: Lowe, a corporate climber who had been moving up the IBM ladder since 1962, soon saw the chance to grab another rung: the sudden heart attack of the head of the Rochester, Minnesota office put him in line for a Vice Presidency. He quickly dropped Project Chess, which had already served his purposes by raising his profile with corporate leadership, to pursue this new opportunity. Responsibility for the development of the IBM personal computer thus fell into the lap of Don Estridge.[8]

sentences.en: Lowe, a corporate climber who had been moving up the IBM ladder since 1962, soon saw the chance to grab another rung: the sudden heart attack of the head of the Rochester, Minnesota office put him in line for a Vice Presidency.
sentences.zh: Lowe 是一位自 1962 年以来一直在 IBM 晋升的企业晋升者，很快就看到了登上另一个台阶的机会：明尼苏达州罗彻斯特办事处负责人突然心脏病发作，使他有机会担任副总裁。

sentences.en: He quickly dropped Project Chess, which had already served his purposes by raising his profile with corporate leadership, to pursue this new opportunity.
sentences.zh: 他很快放弃了国际象棋项目，该项目已经通过提高他在公司领导层中的知名度而达到了他的目的，转而寻求这个新机会。

sentences.en: Responsibility for the development of the IBM personal computer thus fell into the lap of Don Estridge.[8]
sentences.zh: 因此，IBM 个人计算机的开发责任落到了 Don Estridge 的肩上。[8]

blocks.html_en: Slightly older than Lowe, Estridge appeared to have a much less bright future at the company. After stints on the SAGE air defense project and the Apollo project, he eagerly put down roots at the newly opened Boca Raton office in 1969, back in his native state of Florida. Rebelling against the “I’ve Been Moved” company culture, he had no interest in leaving home again to chase promotions. He was also coming off of a rocky “Series 1” minicomputer project that had nearly sunk his career. It was salvaged from financial ruin only by a lucky order from State Farm Insurance.[9]

sentences.en: Slightly older than Lowe, Estridge appeared to have a much less bright future at the company.
sentences.zh: 埃斯特里奇比洛稍稍年长，但他在公司的前途似乎远没有那么光明。

sentences.en: After stints on the SAGE air defense project and the Apollo project, he eagerly put down roots at the newly opened Boca Raton office in 1969, back in his native state of Florida.
sentences.zh: 在 SAGE 防空项目和阿波罗项目工作一段时间后，他热切地在 1969 年新开设的博卡拉顿办事处扎根，回到了他的家乡佛罗里达州。

sentences.en: Rebelling against the “I’ve Been Moved” company culture, he had no interest in leaving home again to chase promotions.
sentences.zh: 由于反抗“我被感动了”的公司文化，他没有兴趣再次离开家去追求升职。

sentences.en: He was also coming off of a rocky “Series 1” minicomputer project that had nearly sunk his career.
sentences.zh: 他还刚刚完成了一个艰难的“系列 1”小型计算机项目，该项目几乎毁掉了他的职业生涯。

sentences.en: It was salvaged from financial ruin only by a lucky order from State Farm Insurance.[9]
sentences.zh: 靠着国家农场保险公司的一份幸运订单，它才从财务灾难中被拯救出来。[9]

blocks.html_en: <figcaption>Don Estridge</figcaption>

sentences.en: Don Estridge
sentences.zh: 唐·埃斯特里奇

blocks.html_en: Nonetheless, Estridge had two qualities that made him perfectly suited to bring Project Chess to fruition. First, the fact that he held the norms of IBM management culture in low regard. He wielded his direct line to the CEO as a talisman to ward off vice-presidential demands and bureaucratic process, and in so doing prevented the lightning sprint to a 1981 launch from bogging down into a multi-year slog. Second, he had insight into the target market because he belonged to it: he had bought an Apple II in 1977 and, like thousands of his fellow hobbyists, tinkered on it in the evenings after work.[10]

sentences.en: Nonetheless, Estridge had two qualities that made him perfectly suited to bring Project Chess to fruition.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，埃斯特里奇有两个品质使他非常适合将国际象棋项目付诸实践。

sentences.en: First, the fact that he held the norms of IBM management culture in low regard.
sentences.zh: 首先，他不尊重 IBM 管理文化的规范。

sentences.en: He wielded his direct line to the CEO as a talisman to ward off vice-presidential demands and bureaucratic process, and in so doing prevented the lightning sprint to a 1981 launch from bogging down into a multi-year slog.
sentences.zh: 他利用与首席执行官的直接沟通作为避开副总统要求和官僚程序的护身符，从而避免了 1981 年推出的闪电冲刺陷入多年的艰难。

sentences.en: Second, he had insight into the target market because he belonged to it: he had bought an Apple II in 1977 and, like thousands of his fellow hobbyists, tinkered on it in the evenings after work.[10]
sentences.zh: 其次，他对目标市场有洞察力，因为他属于这个市场：他在 1977 年买了一台 Apple II，像成千上万的爱好者一样，在晚上下班后对它进行了修修补补。 [10]

blocks.html_en: Estridge protected his people from pressure to use only the IBM dealer network and IBM suppliers. They would sell the PC through third-party retailers—most importantly, ComputerLand. And, though IBM factories were allowed to put in their bids like everyone else, the Acorn would be built mostly from third-party parts: disk drives from Tandon, power supplies from Zenith, printers from Epson, and a processor from Intel. Even after the release of the PC, internal IBM suppliers would be forced to compete with outside vendors for business: to bootstrap an ecosystem as quickly as possible, Project Chess would follow in the footsteps of Apple and publish all the technical specs needed to allow and encourage third parties to write software and create hardware accessories for their new computer.[11]

sentences.en: Estridge protected his people from pressure to use only the IBM dealer network and IBM suppliers.
sentences.zh: Estridge 保护他的员工免受仅使用 IBM 经销商网络和 IBM 供应商的压力。

sentences.en: They would sell the PC through third-party retailers—most importantly, ComputerLand.
sentences.zh: 他们将通过第三方零售商（最重要的是 ComputerLand）销售个人电脑。

sentences.en: And, though IBM factories were allowed to put in their bids like everyone else, the Acorn would be built mostly from third-party parts: disk drives from Tandon, power supplies from Zenith, printers from Epson, and a processor from Intel.
sentences.zh: 而且，尽管 IBM 工厂可以像其他工厂一样参与投标，但 Acorn 将主要由第三方部件制造：Tandon 的磁盘驱动器、Zenith 的电源、Epson 的打印机以及 Intel 的处理器。

sentences.en: Even after the release of the PC, internal IBM suppliers would be forced to compete with outside vendors for business: to bootstrap an ecosystem as quickly as possible, Project Chess would follow in the footsteps of Apple and publish all the technical specs needed to allow and encourage third parties to write software and create hardware accessories for their new computer.[11]
sentences.zh: 即使在 PC 发布之后，IBM 内部供应商也将被迫与外部供应商竞争业务：为了尽快启动一个生态系统，Project Chess 将追随 Apple 的脚步，发布所有所需的技术规范，以允许和鼓励第三方为其新计算机编写软件和创建硬件配件。 [11]

blocks.html_en: Intel was a natural choice for the processor, because many of the engineers in Boca Raton had already become familiar with Intel hardware from the Datamaster project’s 8085. But Estridge’s hardware team wanted to move up to a sixteen-bit processor from the eight-bit processor of the Datamaster (and the vast majority of microcomputers than in use). Doing calculations in sixteen-bit chunks would make the Acorn 1 more efficient at data processing and (most importantly) allow it to support larger amounts of memory. Intel offered the fully-sixteen-bit 8086, but the Chess team chose the hybrid 8088, which processed sixteen-bit chunks internally but communicated with the external word (over its “data bus”) eight bits at a time. This would substantially reduce the overall cost of the machine, since eight-bit support chips were much more common and less expensive to produce.[12]

sentences.en: Intel was a natural choice for the processor, because many of the engineers in Boca Raton had already become familiar with Intel hardware from the Datamaster project’s 8085.
sentences.zh: 英特尔是处理器的自然选择，因为博卡拉顿的许多工程师已经从 Datamaster 项目的 8085 开始熟悉英特尔硬件。

sentences.en: But Estridge’s hardware team wanted to move up to a sixteen-bit processor from the eight-bit processor of the Datamaster (and the vast majority of microcomputers than in use).
sentences.zh: 但埃斯特里奇的硬件团队希望从 Datamaster 的 8 位处理器（以及绝大多数正在使用的微型计算机）升级到 16 位处理器。

sentences.en: Doing calculations in sixteen-bit chunks would make the Acorn 1 more efficient at data processing and (most importantly) allow it to support larger amounts of memory.
sentences.zh: 以 16 位块进行计算将使 Acorn 1 在数据处理方面更加高效，并且（最重要的是）允许它支持更大的内存量。

sentences.en: Intel offered the fully-sixteen-bit 8086, but the Chess team chose the hybrid 8088, which processed sixteen-bit chunks internally but communicated with the external word (over its “data bus”) eight bits at a time.
sentences.zh: Intel 提供了全 16 位 8086，但国际象棋团队选择了混合 8088，它在内部处理 16 位块，但与外部字（通过其“数据总线”）一次进行 8 位通信。

sentences.en: This would substantially reduce the overall cost of the machine, since eight-bit support chips were much more common and less expensive to produce.[12]
sentences.zh: 这将大大降低机器的总体成本，因为八位支持芯片更常见且生产成本更低。 [12]

blocks.html_en: From CP/M to MS-DOS

sentences.en: From CP/M to MS-DOS
sentences.zh: 从 CP/M 到 MS-DOS

blocks.html_en: Even as they were settling these major hardware problems, the Chess team began its search for software. Jack Sams, tasked with heading up this effort, wanted the Acorn to support two modes of operation, both very popular in the personal computing world: running BASIC from read-only memory (ROM) for simple programming tasks and games, and running a disk-based operating system like CP/M from Digital Research for word-processing, accounting, and heavy data-processing tasks.[13]

sentences.en: Even as they were settling these major hardware problems, the Chess team began its search for software.
sentences.zh: 就在他们解决这些主要硬件问题时，国际象棋团队开始寻找软件。

sentences.en: Jack Sams, tasked with heading up this effort, wanted the Acorn to support two modes of operation, both very popular in the personal computing world: running BASIC from read-only memory (ROM) for simple programming tasks and games, and running a disk-based operating system like CP/M from Digital Research for word-processing, accounting, and heavy data-processing tasks.[13]
sentences.zh: Jack Sams 负责领导这项工作，他希望 Acorn 支持两种在个人计算领域非常流行的操作模式：从只读存储器 (ROM) 运行 BASIC 以执行简单的编程任务和游戏，并运行基于磁盘的操作系统，例如 Digital Research 的 CP/M，以执行文字处理、会计和繁重的数据处理任务。 [13]

blocks.html_en: Sams was tasked with finding suppliers for this software, and he went first to a name he already knew and trusted: Microsoft, which provided the BASICs installed on most of the major brand names in the personal computer market. In August 1980, he met with Microsoft’s principals, Bill Gates and Paul Allen, in Seattle. Aware that aligning themselves with IBM could prove very lucrative, they eagerly agreed to supply their BASIC and any other software IBM might require (they ultimately provided several other programming language packages as well as Microsoft’s version of the increasingly musty Colossal Cave Adventure). When asked about licensing CP/M, however, Gates had to regretfully decline. Microsoft had no rights to CP/M, but he was happy to phone up Gary Kildall at Digital Research in Pacific Grove, California, and arrange a meeting.[14]

sentences.en: Sams was tasked with finding suppliers for this software, and he went first to a name he already knew and trusted: Microsoft, which provided the BASICs installed on most of the major brand names in the personal computer market.
sentences.zh: Sams 的任务是寻找该软件的供应商，他首先找到了一个他已经知道并信任的名字：微软，该公司提供了安装在个人计算机市场上大多数主要品牌上的 BASIC。

sentences.en: In August 1980, he met with Microsoft’s principals, Bill Gates and Paul Allen, in Seattle.
sentences.zh: 1980 年 8 月，他在西雅图会见了微软的负责人比尔·盖茨和保罗·艾伦。

sentences.en: Aware that aligning themselves with IBM could prove very lucrative, they eagerly agreed to supply their BASIC and any other software IBM might require (they ultimately provided several other programming language packages as well as Microsoft’s version of the increasingly musty Colossal Cave Adventure).
sentences.zh: 他们意识到与 IBM 结盟可能会带来非常丰厚的利润，因此他们热切地同意提供 BASIC 以及 IBM 可能需要的任何其他软件（他们最终提供了其他几种编程语言包以及 Microsoft 版本的越来越霉味的 Colossal Cave Adventure）。

sentences.en: When asked about licensing CP/M, however, Gates had to regretfully decline.
sentences.zh: 然而，当被问及是否要获得 CP/M 的许可时，盖茨不得不遗憾地拒绝了。

sentences.en: Microsoft had no rights to CP/M, but he was happy to phone up Gary Kildall at Digital Research in Pacific Grove, California, and arrange a meeting.[14]
sentences.zh: 微软没有 CP/M 的权利，但他很乐意给加利福尼亚州太平洋格罗夫数字研究中心的加里·基尔达尔 (Gary Kildall) 打电话，并安排会面。 [14]

blocks.html_en: <figcaption>The house—at the time, Digital Research headquarters—where the meeting on August 22, 1980 took place. [Piotr J. Flatau / CC BY-SA 4.0]</figcaption>

sentences.en: The house—at the time, Digital Research headquarters—where the meeting on August 22, 1980 took place. [Piotr J.
sentences.zh: 这栋房子（当时是数字研究总部）是 1980 年 8 月 22 日举行会议的地方。 [皮奥特·J.

sentences.en: Flatau / CC BY-SA 4.0]
sentences.zh: 弗拉托/CC BY-SA 4.0]

blocks.html_en: Much has been made of this fateful meeting, on August 22, 1980, to which Kildall, unlike the solicitous Gates and Allen, arrived hours late. The negotiations about licensing the yet-to-be-released sixteen-bit CP/M-86 thus fell to his business manager (and wife) Dorothy Kildall. Though the events of that day are much disputed, it is certain that everyone walked away unhappy, and no deal was reached. But IBM had no one else to turn to, and so negotiations continued. The ultimate outcome (a triumph for Microsoft and the eclipse of Digital Research) was determined not by the events of that day, but by the predispositions of the three main parties.[15]

sentences.en: Much has been made of this fateful meeting, on August 22, 1980, to which Kildall, unlike the solicitous Gates and Allen, arrived hours late.
sentences.zh: 1980 年 8 月 22 日，这场决定性的会面已经引起了很多关注，与热心的盖茨和艾伦不同，基尔达尔迟到了几个小时。

sentences.en: The negotiations about licensing the yet-to-be-released sixteen-bit CP/M-86 thus fell to his business manager (and wife) Dorothy Kildall.
sentences.zh: 因此，有关尚未发布的 16 位 CP/M-86 授权的谈判落到了他的业务经理（兼妻子）Dorothy Kildall 的肩上。

sentences.en: Though the events of that day are much disputed, it is certain that everyone walked away unhappy, and no deal was reached.
sentences.zh: 尽管那天发生的事情争议很大，但可以肯定的是，每个人都不满意地离开，并且没有达成任何协议。

sentences.en: But IBM had no one else to turn to, and so negotiations continued.
sentences.zh: 但 IBM 没有其他人可以求助，因此谈判仍在继续。

sentences.en: The ultimate outcome (a triumph for Microsoft and the eclipse of Digital Research) was determined not by the events of that day, but by the predispositions of the three main parties.[15]
sentences.zh: 最终的结果（微软的胜利和数字研究的黯然失色）不是由那天发生的事件决定的，而是由三个主要各方的倾向决定的。 [15]

blocks.html_en: Sams disliked the standoffish attitude of Digital Research and had wanted, in the first place, to deal only with the supplier he already trusted: Microsoft. As for Gates and Kildall, though they both loved computers, and had once briefly considered a merger of their companies, they had very different personalities. Gates had been angling to build a business since he was in high school; he and Allen created Microsoft to meet a market need: a BASIC interpreter for the Altair, which they believe would sell like hotcakes. Kildall had created CP/M as the incidental byproduct of a hobby interest: his desired to write PL/M code on a microcomputer. For better or worse, he lacked Gates’ relentlessness and drive. He certainly wanted to do well for himself, but he cared more about building interesting software than winning in the market, and he disliked IBM’s attitude more than he liked their money. The Digital Research of the 1980s would never have become the Microsoft of the 1990s, no matter what happened on August 22nd.

sentences.zh: Sams 不喜欢 Digital Research 的冷漠态度，并且首先希望只与他已经信任的供应商打交道：微软。

blocks.html_en: The facts of how these dynamics played out were as follows: a frustrated Sams went back to Gates to ask again about a disk operating system. Through Allen, Gates knew of one other possibility: a 16-bit CP/M clone created by a local company, Seattle Computer Products (SCP). In 1979, SCP had started selling an S-100 compatible motherboard for the Intel 8086 processor, designed by employee Tim Paterson. But unlike in the ancient of the Altair, by 1979 most hobbyists expected to have a disk operating system available for their computer. So, Paterson wrote one, which he called Quick and Dirty Operating System (QDOS), and which SCP sold externally as 86-DOS. It was a near-perfect clone of CP/M for the 8086. Because the 8086 and 8088 used identical machine code, this meant it would also work on the IBM personal computer.[16]

sentences.en: The facts of how these dynamics played out were as follows: a frustrated Sams went back to Gates to ask again about a disk operating system.
sentences.zh: 这些动态如何发挥作用的事实如下：沮丧的萨姆斯回到盖茨再次询问有关磁盘操作系统的问题。

blocks.html_en: Gates offered to put Sams in touch with SCP, but the dealings with Digital Research had already exhausted his patience with microcomputer software makers. He asked Gates to do the deal himself and license 86-DOS to IBM. Gates duly did so (all the while concealing the existence of his deep-pocketed customer from SCP) and then worked diligently to make sure Microsoft could deliver exactly what IBM wanted, in plenty of time for the 1981 PC launch—he even hired Paterson away from SCP to polish 86-DOS into what IBM would call PC-DOS. But the crucial moment for Microsoft came in July 1981, when Gates convinced SCP’s owner to sell him all the rights to 86-DOS, in exchange for $50,000 and a perpetual license back to SCP. Because IBM had not secured an exclusive license to PC-DOS, Microsoft could now sell it to others, giving this same piece of software its fourth name, MS-DOS. This deal was the basis for Microsoft’s future multi-billion-dollar empire.[17]

blocks.html_en: With Digital Research threatening to sue over the similarities between PC-DOS and CP/M-86, IBM agreed to offer the latter as an additional operating system for the IBM PC. But this alternative did not actually become available until April 1982, months after IBM launched its computer, and because Digital Research demanded per-copy royalties (while Microsoft licensed DOS for a flat fee), it sold for $240, six times as much as PC-DOS. This was not the end of Digital Research, which found a niche in the operating system market and survived until 1991, when another software maker, Novell offered $80 million to buy the company. The previous quarter, Microsoft had made $140 million in profit.[18]

sentences.zh: 由于 Digital Research 威胁要起诉 PC-DOS 和 CP/M-86 之间的相似性，IBM 同意提供后者作为 IBM PC 的附加操作系统。

blocks.html_en: The IBM PC debuted on August 12, 1981, in a press conference at New York’s Waldorf Astoria hotel, almost exactly a year after Lowe had committed to delivering it. The Chess team had estimated one million units in sales in the first three years, but corporate planning, unable to credit such a figure, cut the target down to 200,000, which restricted supply in the back half of 1981. Nonetheless, after three years, the PC was right on target for one million sold. Estridge rose from obscurity to become an overnight star: having “delivered nearly $1 billion of revenue, that, not being in anyone’s business plan, appeared to come out of thin air… Estridge’s bosses looked at him as something of a magician.”[19]

blocks.html_en: An ungenerous historian might credit the PC’s success solely to the IBM name: businesspeople skeptical of the microcomputer fad (or uncertain whether existing brands could be trusted to last) felt comfortable betting on a machine to which this venerable, trusted brand had lent its credit. And there is certainly truth to that. As soon as he heard the IBM PC was coming, Martin Alpert, president of a small technology company in Cleveland, shifted his whole business on making add-ons for the computer. He spoke for third-party vendors as a whole when he said that “people wanted a standard. They didn’t know what to buy until they heard about the IBM PC and they figured they couldn’t go wrong because it was IBM. As soon as we saw the machine, we believed in it and we bet the future of our company on it.”[20]

sentences.en: An ungenerous historian might credit the PC’s success solely to the IBM name: businesspeople skeptical of the microcomputer fad (or uncertain whether existing brands could be trusted to last) felt comfortable betting on a machine to which this venerable, trusted brand had lent its credit.
sentences.zh: 一位吝啬的历史学家可能会将 PC 的成功仅仅归功于 IBM 的名字：对微型计算机的流行持怀疑态度（或不确定现有品牌是否可以持久）的商人放心地将赌注押在这个受人尊敬、值得信赖的品牌所信赖的机器上。

blocks.html_en: Yet it is also true that the PC would never have succeeded so well had it not also been an excellent, forward-looking design, which combined many of the best features of its competitors. As BYTE’s senior editor wrote, it had “color graphics like the Apple II, an 80-column display like the TRS-80 Model II, a redefinable character set like the Atari 800, a 16-bit microprocessor like the Texas Instruments TI 99/4, an expanded memory space like the Apple III, a full-function uppercase and lowercase keyboard like the TRS-80 Model III, and BASIC color graphics like the TRS-80 Color Computer…”[21]

sentences.en: Yet it is also true that the PC would never have succeeded so well had it not also been an excellent, forward-looking design, which combined many of the best features of its competitors.
sentences.zh: 然而，如果不是出色的、前瞻性的设计，结合了竞争对手的许多最佳功能，那么 PC 永远不会取得如此大的成功，这也是事实。

sentences.en: As BYTE’s senior editor wrote, it had “color graphics like the Apple II, an 80-column display like the TRS-80 Model II, a redefinable character set like the Atari 800, a 16-bit microprocessor like the Texas Instruments TI 99/4, an expanded memory space like the Apple III, a full-function uppercase and lowercase keyboard like the TRS-80 Model III, and BASIC color graphics like the TRS-80 Color Computer…”[21]
sentences.zh: 正如 BYTE 的高级编辑所写，它具有“像 Apple II 一样的彩色图形、像 TRS-80 Model II 一样的 80 列显示屏、像 Atari 800 一样的可重新定义字符集、像德州仪器 TI 99/4 一样的 16 位微处理器、像 Apple III 一样的扩展内存空间、像 TRS-80 Model III 一样的全功能大写和小写键盘，以及像 TRS-80 Color 一样的 BASIC 彩色图形。计算机……”[21]

blocks.html_en: The most important of these features were the sixteen-bit microprocessor and the expanded memory access, which went hand in hand. Most 8-bit computers could read from or write to a maximum of sixty-four kilobytes of memory (by combining two eight-bit addresses). The Apple III had quadrupled this with a technique called bank-switching, but the IBM, with sixteen-bit addresses plus a four-bit segment identifier, could access an entire megabyte (though applications could access only 640 kilobytes—later a notorious number—with the rest reserved for system software and input/output devices). One would be hard-pressed to find a microcomputer user in possession of so much memory in 1981, but with prices falling and application software growing ever more sophisticated, business users would soon both want that much memory and be able to afford it, and the PC was well-prepared for that eventuality.[22]

sentences.en: The most important of these features were the sixteen-bit microprocessor and the expanded memory access, which went hand in hand.
sentences.zh: 这些功能中最重要的是十六位微处理器和扩展的内存访问，它们是相辅相成的。

sentences.en: Most 8-bit computers could read from or write to a maximum of sixty-four kilobytes of memory (by combining two eight-bit addresses).
sentences.zh: 大多数 8 位计算机最多可以读取或写入 64 KB 的内存（通过组合两个 8 位地址）。

sentences.en: The Apple III had quadrupled this with a technique called bank-switching, but the IBM, with sixteen-bit addresses plus a four-bit segment identifier, could access an entire megabyte (though applications could access only 640 kilobytes—later a notorious number—with the rest reserved for system software and input/output devices).
sentences.zh: Apple III 通过一种称为组交换的技术将其增加了四倍，但 IBM 具有 16 位地址加上 4 位段标识符，可以访问整个兆字节（尽管应用程序只能访问 640 KB——后来成为一个臭名昭著的数字——其余的保留给系统软件和输入/输出设备）。

sentences.en: One would be hard-pressed to find a microcomputer user in possession of so much memory in 1981, but with prices falling and application software growing ever more sophisticated, business users would soon both want that much memory and be able to afford it, and the PC was well-prepared for that eventuality.[22]
sentences.zh: 在 1981 年，很难找到一个微型计算机用户拥有如此大的内存，但随着价格的下降和应用软件变得越来越复杂，商业用户很快就会既想要那么多的内存，又能够负担得起，而 PC 已经为这种可能性做好了充分的准备。 [22]

blocks.html_en: By the spring of 1983, IBM’s personal computer sales already matched those of Apple and Tandy. Andrew Pollack of the New York Times noted that “an entire subindustry has sprung up to supply parts, accessories and advice for the I.B.M. personal computer,” and that “I.B.M.’s role in the personal computer world is beginning to resemble its central role in the mainframe computer business, in which I.B.M. is the sun around which everything else revolves.” A new catalog of IBM-specific literature sprung up to serve this new market: David Bunnell, who had left MITS (creator of the ALtair) to found Personal Computing, now became editor-in-chief for PC Magazine, entirely dedicated to IBM’s new computer. Many brands (from Dumpster to Kleenex) had found their proprietary name transformed into a generic label, but IBM had managed to turn the previously generic term “personal computer” into the proprietary acronym “PC.”[23]

blocks.html_en: The PC’s open architecture, borrowed from minicomputers via Apple and the Altair-compatibles, attracted software and hardware vendors and kicked off a new flywheel of success even more powerful than the one that propelled the Apple II. As much from necessity as from calculation, Estridge’s team had blended the open traditions of the personal computer and the authority of IBM’s imprimatur into an enticing cocktail. Both conformists and rebels could find something appealing in its flavor. The marketing campaign struck the same chords: “My own IBM computer. Imagine that,” went one tagline. Now you could have complete control over your own computer without giving up the assurance of the IBM brand.[24]

sentences.en: The PC’s open architecture, borrowed from minicomputers via Apple and the Altair-compatibles, attracted software and hardware vendors and kicked off a new flywheel of success even more powerful than the one that propelled the Apple II.
sentences.zh: PC 的开放式架构通过 Apple 和 Altair 兼容机借鉴自小型机，吸引了软件和硬件供应商，并开启了新的成功飞轮，甚至比推动 Apple II 的飞轮更强大。

sentences.en: Imagine that,” went one tagline.
sentences.zh: 想象一下，”一个口号这样说道。

sentences.en: Now you could have complete control over your own computer without giving up the assurance of the IBM brand.[24]
sentences.zh: 现在您可以完全控制自己的计算机，而无需放弃 IBM 品牌的保证。[24]

blocks.html_en: Yet, this concoction that so enchanted its drinkers also proved dangerous to the brewer. An open architecture was more easily copied, and by March 1983, more than 20 “clones” of the IBM PC already existed, “interchangeable or almost interchangeable with the I.B.M. computer.” Internally, IBM executives seethed at the press focus on Don Estridge, who generate only a small fraction of IBM’s revenue while hurting the rest of the company “by not trying to tie his products into the rest of the company’s, by not working with the IBM sales force, or by not being willing to buy his parts from IBM.” In short order, the full force of internal politics was unleashed on the formerly sheltered PC group. The immediate successor models to the PC, 1983’s XT and 1984’s AT, both sold terrifically, but darker times loomed ahead. After its magician died in a horrific plane crash in Dallas in 1985, IBM would dig deep into its hat but never produce another rabbit.[25]
